name: Gary McFarland
abstract: Gary Ronald McFarland (October 23, 1933 – November 2, 1971) was an American composer, arranger, conductor, vibraphonist, and vocalist. He recorded for the jazz imprints Verve and Impulse!

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Salute to Eddie Condon

about_quotes.speaker: Michael Abene
about_quotes.evidence: I wrote with Gary when he used to have a company called Sky Records years ago... I did Grady Tate’s first vocal album “Windmills of your Mind.” I did one side and Gary did one side.
about_quotes.doc_title: Michael Abene interviewed by Monk Rowe, Toronto, Canada, January 11, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Holman Bill
about_quotes.evidence: Gerry called up and wanted to hang out... That was the start of our friendship... he asked me to come back and help him get the music ready... Brookmeyer and Al Cohn and Gary McFarland, all those guys, were writing anything they wanted to
about_quotes.doc_title: Holman Bill — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Terry Clark
about_quotes.evidence: I know he loved Ellington, because we used to talk about it all the time... the other dates that we did after that date
about_quotes.doc_title: Terry Clark — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: David N. Baker
about_quotes.evidence: It had the vibe player who died... Gary McFarland. Gary was there as a student. We were all students.
about_quotes.doc_title: David N. Baker — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Marty Morell
about_quotes.evidence: we had just done an album with Gary McFarland, a thing called “October Suite.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Marty Morell interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 24, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Sauter
about_quotes.evidence: I heard Gary McFarland, but I don't really recall anything like that.
